{{Infobox historic site | name = Shimosamuraizuka Kofun | native_name = 下侍塚古墳 | native_language = ja | image = [[File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-3.jpg|280px]]
Mound (foreground: anterior section, background: posterior section) | other_name = Kurumazuka | location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|36|48|46.75|N|140|7|22.35|E|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Anterior-posterior burial mound|ja|前方後方墳}} | built = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} | materials = {{ill|Clay-coffin burial|ja|粘土槨}} | excavation = 1692 (Edo period), 1975 | designation1 = National Historic Site of Japan | designation1_date = 1951 }} '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' (下侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後方墳}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site under the name {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun|ja|侍塚古墳}}. The tumulus is notable as one of the first burial mounds to be subjected to an academic archaeological investigation in Japan, conducted during the Edo period under the orders of Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Mito Kōmon). This article also covers the {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}}, a collection of tumuli near Shimosamuraizuka Kofun that is designated as a municipal historic site. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Major Anterior-Posterior Burial Mounds in the Nasu Region ! colspan=3 | Tumulus Groups ! Tumulus Name ! Size ! Anterior Orientation ! Paving Stones ! Burial Method |- | rowspan=3 colspan=3 | || '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' || 84 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}} || 114 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun|ja|上侍塚北古墳}} || 48.5 m || South || None || Unknown |}
Shimosamuraizuka Kofun is located on a terrace on the right bank of the Naka River in northeastern Tochigi Prefecture. In addition to the Edo period excavation in 1692, another investigation was conducted in 1975. The tumulus is an anterior-posterior burial mound with the anterior section oriented to the south. Measuring 84 meters in length, it is the second largest of the six anterior-posterior burial mounds in the Nasu region. The mound is surrounded by a moat, where artifacts such as a red-painted earthenware jar with a perforated base have been discovered.{{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=1988|chapter=Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Place Names Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 9 Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490093}} The burial chamber, located at the top of the posterior section, is a simple clay-coffin burial with clay blocks placed at both ends. Artifacts recovered during the Edo period excavation include bronze mirrors (a Two Gods and Two Beasts Mirror), iron implements (15 sword fragments, armor pieces, a sword hilt, and a spearhead), and earthenware (four tall cups and a jar). The tumulus dates to the end of the 4th century, during the early Middle Kofun period. Following the Edo period investigation, pine trees were planted to protect the mound, making Shimosamuraizuka Kofun one of the earliest examples of cultural heritage preservation in Japan. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" designation.{{Cite web|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/|publisher=National Cultural Property Database}} == Mound Details == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun Information Board. * Total length: 84 meters * Posterior section: * Length: 48 meters * Width: 48 meters * Height: 9.4 meters * Anterior section: * Length: 36 meters * Width: 36 meters * Height: 5 meters == Samuraizuka Kofun Group == {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}} is a collection of burial mounds located northwest of Shimosamuraizuka Kofun. While around 10 mounds originally existed, only eight remain today and have been designated as municipal historic sites. The group consists of one anterior-posterior burial mound, six round mounds, and one square mound. Partial excavations have been conducted on mounds 5 and 8. File:Samuraizuka Kofun Group, gaikan.jpg|Overview of the Samuraizuka Kofun Group File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun artifacts.jpg|Artifacts from the Samuraizuka Kofun Group, displayed at the Ōtawara City Museum. == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * Samuraizuka Kofun (includes Shimosamuraizuka and Kamisamuraizuka Kofun) – Designated on June 9, 1951. === Municipal Historic Sites === * Samuraizuka Kofun Group – Designated on February 15, 1966.[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082775061/ Municipal Historic Sites List, Ōtawara City]. == Related Facilities == * Ōtawara City Nasu Fudoki-no-Oka Museum – Houses and displays artifacts from Shimosamuraizuka Kofun and the Samuraizuka Kofun Group. == References == {{reflist}} == Related Reading == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=2013|chapter=|title=The Story of Nasu Fudoki – Exploring the Ancient Burial Mounds of Nasu|publisher=Ōtawara City Nasu Fudoki-no-Oka Museum}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun|ja|侍塚古墳}} == External Links == * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] – Ōtawara City * {{Commonscat|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun}} [[Category:Kofun in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ōtawara]] [[Category:Anterior-posterior burial mounds]] [[Category:National Historic Sites of Japan in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[ja:下侍塚古墳]]

While around 10 mounds originally existed, only eight remain today and have been designated as municipal historic sites. Together with {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site under the name {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun|ja|侍塚古墳}}. The tumulus is notable as one of the first burial mounds to be subjected to an academic archaeological investigation in Japan, conducted during the Edo period under the orders of Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Mito Kōmon). Shimosamuraizuka Kofun is located on a terrace on the right bank of the Naka River in northeastern Tochigi Prefecture. In addition to the Edo period excavation in 1692, another investigation was conducted in 1975. The tumulus is an anterior-posterior burial mound with the anterior section oriented to the south. Measuring 84 meters in length, it is the second largest of the six anterior-posterior burial mounds in the Nasu region. The mound is surrounded by a moat, where artifacts such as a red-painted earthenware jar with a perforated base have been discovered.{{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=1988|chapter=Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Place Names Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 9 Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490093}} The burial chamber, located at the top of the posterior section, is a simple clay-coffin burial with clay blocks placed at both ends. Artifacts recovered during the Edo period excavation include bronze mirrors (a Two Gods and Two Beasts Mirror), iron implements (15 sword fragments, armor pieces, a sword hilt, and a spearhead), and earthenware (four tall cups and a jar). The tumulus dates to the end of the 4th century, during the early Middle Kofun period. Following the Edo period investigation, pine trees were planted to protect the mound, making Shimosamuraizuka Kofun one of the earliest examples of cultural heritage preservation in Japan. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" designation.{{Cite web|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/|publisher=National Cultural Property Database}} == Mound Details == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun Information Board. * Total length: 84 meters * Posterior section: * Length: 48 meters * Width: 48 meters * Height: 9.4 meters * Anterior section: * Length: 36 meters * Width: 36 meters * Height: 5 meters == Samuraizuka Kofun Group == {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}} is a collection of burial mounds located northwest of Shimosamuraizuka Kofun. The group consists of one anterior-posterior burial mound, six round mounds, and one square mound. Partial excavations have been conducted on mounds 5 and 8. {{Infobox historic site | name = Shimosamuraizuka Kofun | native_name = 下侍塚古墳 | native_language = ja | image = [[File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-3.jpg|280px]]
Mound (foreground: anterior section, background: posterior section) | other_name = Kurumazuka | location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|36|48|46.75|N|140|7|22.35|E|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳}} | built = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} | materials = {{ill|Clay-coffin burial|ja|粘土槨}} | excavation = 1692 (Edo period), 1975 | designation1 = National Historic Site of Japan | designation1_date = 1951 }} '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' (下侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後方墳}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site under the name {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun|ja|侍塚古墳}}. The tumulus is notable as one of the first burial mounds to be subjected to an academic archaeological investigation in Japan, conducted during the Edo period under the orders of Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Mito Kōmon). This article also covers the {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}}, a collection of tumuli near Shimosamuraizuka Kofun that is designated as a municipal historic site. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Major Anterior-Posterior Burial Mounds in the Nasu Region ! colspan=3 | Tumulus Groups ! Tumulus Name ! Size ! Anterior Orientation ! Paving Stones ! Burial Method |- | rowspan=3 colspan=3 | || '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' || 84 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}} || 114 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun|ja|上侍塚北古墳}} || 48.5 m || South || None || Unknown |}
Shimosamuraizuka Kofun is located on a terrace on the right bank of the Naka River in northeastern Tochigi Prefecture. In addition to the Edo period excavation in 1692, another investigation was conducted in 1975. The tumulus is an anterior-posterior burial mound with the anterior section oriented to the south. Measuring 84 meters in length, it is the second largest of the six anterior-posterior burial mounds in the Nasu region. The mound is surrounded by a moat, where artifacts such as a red-painted earthenware jar with a perforated base have been discovered.{{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=1988|chapter=Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Place Names Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 9 Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490093}} The burial chamber, located at the top of the posterior section, is a simple clay-coffin burial with clay blocks placed at both ends. Artifacts recovered during the Edo period excavation include bronze mirrors (a Two Gods and Two Beasts Mirror), iron implements (15 sword fragments, armor pieces, a sword hilt, and a spearhead), and earthenware (four tall cups and a jar). The tumulus dates to the end of the 4th century, during the early Middle Kofun period. Following the Edo period investigation, pine trees were planted to protect the mound, making Shimosamuraizuka Kofun one of the earliest examples of cultural heritage preservation in Japan. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" designation.{{Cite web|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/|publisher=National Cultural Property Database}} == Mound Details == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun Information Board. * Total length: 84 meters * Posterior section: * Length: 48 meters * Width: 48 meters * Height: 9.4 meters * Anterior section: * Length: 36 meters * Width: 36 meters * Height: 5 meters == Samuraizuka Kofun Group == {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}} is a collection of burial mounds located northwest of Shimosamuraizuka Kofun. While around 10 mounds originally existed, only eight remain today and have been designated as municipal historic sites. The group consists of one anterior-posterior burial mound, six round mounds, and one square mound. Partial excavations have been conducted on mounds 5 and 8. {{Infobox historic site | name = Shimosamuraizuka Kofun | native_name = 下侍塚古墳 | native_language = ja | image = [[File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-3.jpg|280px]]
Mound (foreground: anterior section, background: posterior section) | other_name = Kurumazuka | location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|36|48|46.75|N|140|7|22.35|E|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳}} | built = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} | materials = {{ill|Clay-coffin burial|ja|粘土槨}} | excavation = 1692 (Edo period), 1975 | designation1 = National Historic Site of Japan | designation1_date = 1951 }} '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' (下侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後方墳}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site under the name {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun|ja|侍塚古墳}}. The tumulus is notable as one of the first burial mounds to be subjected to an academic archaeological investigation in Japan, conducted during the Edo period under the orders of Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Mito Kōmon). This article also covers the {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}}, a collection of tumuli near Shimosamuraizuka Kofun that is designated as a municipal historic site. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Major Anterior-Posterior Burial Mounds in the Nasu Region ! colspan=3 | Tumulus Groups ! Tumulus Name ! Size ! Anterior Orientation ! Paving Stones ! Burial Method |- | rowspan=3 colspan=3 | || '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' || 84 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}} || 114 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun|ja|上侍塚北古墳}} || 48.5 m || South || None || Unknown |}
Shimosamuraizuka Kofun is located on a terrace on the right bank of the Naka River in northeastern Tochigi Prefecture. In addition to the Edo period excavation in 1692, another investigation was conducted in 1975. The tumulus is an anterior-posterior burial mound with the anterior section oriented to the south. Measuring 84 meters in length, it is the second largest of the six anterior-posterior burial mounds in the Nasu region. The mound is surrounded by a moat, where artifacts such as a red-painted earthenware jar with a perforated base have been discovered.{{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=1988|chapter=Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Place Names Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 9 Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490093}} The burial chamber, located at the top of the posterior section, is a simple clay-coffin burial with clay blocks placed at both ends. Artifacts recovered during the Edo period excavation include bronze mirrors (a Two Gods and Two Beasts Mirror), iron implements (15 sword fragments, armor pieces, a sword hilt, and a spearhead), and earthenware (four tall cups and a jar). The tumulus dates to the end of the 4th century, during the early Middle Kofun period. Following the Edo period investigation, pine trees were planted to protect the mound, making Shimosamuraizuka Kofun one of the earliest examples of cultural heritage preservation in Japan. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" designation.{{Cite web|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/|publisher=National Cultural Property Database}} == Mound Details == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun Information Board. * Total length: 84 meters * Posterior section: * Length: 48 meters * Width: 48 meters * Height: 9.4 meters * Anterior section: * Length: 36 meters * Width: 36 meters * Height: 5 meters == Samuraizuka Kofun Group == {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}} is a collection of burial mounds located northwest of Shimosamuraizuka Kofun. While around 10 mounds originally existed, only eight remain today and have been designated as municipal historic sites. The group consists of one anterior-posterior burial mound, six round mounds, and one square mound. Partial excavations have been conducted on mounds 5 and 8. {{Infobox historic site | name = Shimosamuraizuka Kofun | native_name = 下侍塚古墳 | native_language = ja | image = [[File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-3.jpg|280px]]
Mound (foreground: anterior section, background: posterior section) | other_name = Kurumazuka | location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|36|48|46.75|N|140|7|22.35|E|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳}} | built = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} | materials = {{ill|Clay-coffin burial|ja|粘土槨}} | excavation = 1692 (Edo period), 1975 | designation1 = National Historic Site of Japan | designation1_date = 1951 }} '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' (下侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後方墳}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site under the name {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun|ja|侍塚古墳}}. The tumulus is notable as one of the first burial mounds to be subjected to an academic archaeological investigation in Japan, conducted during the Edo period under the orders of Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Mito Kōmon). This article also covers the {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}}, a collection of tumuli near Shimosamuraizuka Kofun that is designated as a municipal historic site. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Major Anterior-Posterior Burial Mounds in the Nasu Region ! colspan=3 | Tumulus Groups ! Tumulus Name ! Size ! Anterior Orientation ! Paving Stones ! Burial Method |- | rowspan=3 colspan=3 | || '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' || 84 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}} || 114 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun|ja|上侍塚北古墳}} || 48.5 m || South || None || Unknown |}
Shimosamuraizuka Kofun is located on a terrace on the right bank of the Naka River in northeastern Tochigi Prefecture. In addition to the Edo period excavation in 1692, another investigation was conducted in 1975. The tumulus is an anterior-posterior burial mound with the anterior section oriented to the south. Measuring 84 meters in length, it is the second largest of the six anterior-posterior burial mounds in the Nasu region. The mound is surrounded by a moat, where artifacts such as a red-painted earthenware jar with a perforated base have been discovered.{{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=1988|chapter=Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Place Names Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 9 Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490093}} The burial chamber, located at the top of the posterior section, is a simple clay-coffin burial with clay blocks placed at both ends. Artifacts recovered during the Edo period excavation include bronze mirrors (a Two Gods and Two Beasts Mirror), iron implements (15 sword fragments, armor pieces, a sword hilt, and a spearhead), and earthenware (four tall cups and a jar). The tumulus dates to the end of the 4th century, during the early Middle Kofun period. Following the Edo period investigation, pine trees were planted to protect the mound, making Shimosamuraizuka Kofun one of the earliest examples of cultural heritage preservation in Japan. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" designation.{{Cite web|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/|publisher=National Cultural Property Database}} == Mound Details == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun Information Board. * Total length: 84 meters * Posterior section: * Length: 48 meters * Width: 48 meters * Height: 9.4 meters * Anterior section: * Length: 36 meters * Width: 36 meters * Height: 5 meters == Samuraizuka Kofun Group == {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}} is a collection of burial mounds located northwest of Shimosamuraizuka Kofun. While around 10 mounds originally existed, only eight remain today and have been designated as municipal historic sites. The group consists of one anterior-posterior burial mound, six round mounds, and one square mound. Partial excavations have been conducted on mounds 5 and 8. {{Infobox historic site | name = Shimosamuraizuka Kofun | native_name = 下侍塚古墳 | native_language = ja | image = [[File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-3.jpg|280px]]
Mound (foreground: anterior section, background: posterior section) | other_name = Kurumazuka | location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|36|48|46.75|N|140|7|22.35|E|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳}} | built = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} | materials = {{ill|Clay-coffin burial|ja|粘土槨}} | excavation = 1692 (Edo period), 1975 | designation1 = National Historic Site of Japan | designation1_date = 1951 }} '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' (下侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後方墳}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site under the name {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun|ja|侍塚古墳}}. The tumulus is notable as one of the first burial mounds to be subjected to an academic archaeological investigation in Japan, conducted during the Edo period under the orders of Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Mito Kōmon). This article also covers the {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}}, a collection of tumuli near Shimosamuraizuka Kofun that is designated as a municipal historic site. It is known as "the most beautiful kofun in Japan"{{ill|Mori Kōichi|ja|森浩一}}, ''Excavation of Kofun,'' Chūō Kōron Shinsho, 1965.. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Major Anterior-Posterior Burial Mounds in the Nasu Region ! colspan=3 | Tumulus Groups ! Tumulus Name ! Size ! Anterior Orientation ! Paving Stones ! Burial Method |- | rowspan=3 colspan=3 | || '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' || 84 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}} || 114 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun|ja|上侍塚北古墳}} || 48.5 m || South || None || Unknown |}
Shimosamuraizuka Kofun is located on a terrace on the right bank of the Naka River in northeastern Tochigi Prefecture. In addition to the Edo period excavation in 1692, another investigation was conducted in 1975. The tumulus is an anterior-posterior burial mound with the anterior section oriented to the south. Measuring 84 meters in length, it is the second largest of the six anterior-posterior burial mounds in the Nasu region. The mound is surrounded by a moat, where artifacts such as a red-painted earthenware jar with a perforated base have been discovered.{{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=1988|chapter=Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Place Names Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 9 Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490093}} The burial chamber, located at the top of the posterior section, is a simple clay-coffin burial with clay blocks placed at both ends. Artifacts recovered during the Edo period excavation include bronze mirrors (a Two Gods and Two Beasts Mirror), iron implements (15 sword fragments, armor pieces, a sword hilt, and a spearhead), and earthenware (four tall cups and a jar). The tumulus dates to the end of the 4th century, during the early Middle Kofun period. Following the Edo period investigation, pine trees were planted to protect the mound, making Shimosamuraizuka Kofun one of the earliest examples of cultural heritage preservation in Japan. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" designation.{{Cite web|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/|publisher=National Cultural Property Database}} == Mound Details == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun Information Board. * Total length: 84 meters * Posterior section: * Length: 48 meters * Width: 48 meters * Height: 9.4 meters * Anterior section: * Length: 36 meters * Width: 36 meters * Height: 5 meters == Samuraizuka Kofun Group == {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}} is a collection of burial mounds located northwest of Shimosamuraizuka Kofun. While around 10 mounds originally existed, only eight remain today and have been designated as municipal historic sites. The group consists of one anterior-posterior burial mound, six round mounds, and one square mound. Partial excavations have been conducted on mounds 5 and 8. While around 10 mounds originally existed, only eight remain today and have been designated as municipal historic sites. The group consists of one anterior-posterior burial mound, six round mounds, and one square mound. Partial excavations have been conducted on mounds 5 and 8. {{Infobox historic site | name = Shimosamuraizuka Kofun | native_name = 下侍塚古墳 | native_language = ja | image = [[File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-3.jpg|280px]]
Mound (foreground: anterior section, background: posterior section) | other_name = Kurumazuka | location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|36|48|46.75|N|140|7|22.35|E|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳}} | built = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} | materials = {{ill|Clay-coffin burial|ja|粘土槨}} | excavation = 1692 (Edo period), 1975 | designation1 = National Historic Site of Japan | designation1_date = 1951 }} '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' (下侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後方墳}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site under the name {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun|ja|侍塚古墳}}. The tumulus is notable as one of the first burial mounds to be subjected to an academic archaeological investigation in Japan, conducted during the Edo period under the orders of Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Mito Kōmon). This article also covers the {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}}, a collection of tumuli near Shimosamuraizuka Kofun that is designated as a municipal historic site. It is known as "the most beautiful kofun in Japan"{{ill|Mori Kōichi|ja|森浩一}}, ''Excavation of Kofun,'' Chūō Kōron Shinsho, 1965.. == Overview ==

Shimosamuraizuka Kofun is located on a terrace on the right bank of the Naka River in northeastern Tochigi Prefecture. In addition to the Edo period excavation in 1692, another investigation was conducted in 1975. The tumulus is an anterior-posterior burial mound with the anterior section oriented to the south. Measuring 84 meters in length, it is the second largest of the six anterior-posterior burial mounds in the Nasu region. The mound is surrounded by a moat, where artifacts such as a red-painted earthenware jar with a perforated base have been discovered.{{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=1988|chapter=Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Place Names Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 9 Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490093}} The burial chamber, located at the top of the posterior section, is a simple clay-coffin burial with clay blocks placed at both ends. Artifacts recovered during the Edo period excavation include bronze mirrors (a Two Gods and Two Beasts Mirror), iron implements (15 sword fragments, armor pieces, a sword hilt, and a spearhead), and earthenware (four tall cups and a jar). The tumulus dates to the end of the 4th century, during the early Middle Kofun period. Following the Edo period investigation, pine trees were planted to protect the mound, making Shimosamuraizuka Kofun one of the earliest examples of cultural heritage preservation in Japan. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" designation.{{Cite web|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/|publisher=National Cultural Property Database}} == Mound Details == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun Information Board. * Total length: 84 meters * Posterior section: ** Length: 48 meters ** Width: 48 meters ** Height: 9.4 meters * Anterior section: ** Length: 36 meters ** Width: 36 meters ** Height: 5 meters == Samuraizuka Kofun Group == {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}} is a collection of burial mounds located northwest of Shimosamuraizuka Kofun. While around 10 mounds originally existed, only eight remain today and have been designated as municipal historic sites. The group consists of one anterior-posterior burial mound, six round mounds, and one square mound. Partial excavations have been conducted on mounds 5 and 8. The mound is surrounded by a moat, where artifacts such as a red-painted earthenware jar with a perforated base have been discovered.{{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=1988|chapter=Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Place Names Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 9 Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490093}} The burial chamber, located at the top of the posterior section, is a simple clay-coffin burial with clay blocks placed at both ends. Artifacts recovered during the Edo period excavation include bronze mirrors (a Two Gods and Two Beasts Mirror), iron implements (15 sword fragments, armor pieces, a sword hilt, and a spearhead), and earthenware (four tall cups and a jar). The tumulus dates to the end of the 4th century, during the early Middle Kofun period. Following the Edo period investigation, pine trees were planted to protect the mound, making Shimosamuraizuka Kofun one of the earliest examples of cultural heritage preservation in Japan. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" designation.{{Cite web|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/|publisher=National Cultural Property Database}} == Mound Details == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun Information Board. * Total length: 84 meters * Posterior section: ** Length: 48 meters ** Width: 48 meters ** Height: 9.4 meters * Anterior section: ** Length: 36 meters ** Width: 36 meters ** Height: 5 meters == Samuraizuka Kofun Group == {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}} is a collection of burial mounds located northwest of Shimosamuraizuka Kofun. While around 10 mounds originally existed, only eight remain today and have been designated as municipal historic sites. The group consists of one anterior-posterior burial mound, six round mounds, and one square mound. Partial excavations have been conducted on mounds 5 and 8. {{Infobox historic site | name = Shimosamuraizuka Kofun | native_name = 下侍塚古墳 | native_language = ja | image = [[File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-3.jpg|280px]]
Mound (foreground: anterior section, background: posterior section) | other_name = Kurumazuka | location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|36|48|46.75|N|140|7|22.35|E|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳}} | built = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} | materials = {{ill|Clay-coffin burial|ja|粘土槨}} | excavation = 1692 (Edo period), 1975 | designation1 = National Historic Site of Japan | designation1_date = 1951 }} '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' (下侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後方墳}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site under the name {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun|ja|侍塚古墳}}. The tumulus is notable as one of the first burial mounds to be subjected to an academic archaeological investigation in Japan, conducted during the Edo period under the orders of Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Mito Kōmon). This article also covers the {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}}, a collection of tumuli near Shimosamuraizuka Kofun that is designated as a municipal historic site. It is known as "the most beautiful kofun in Japan"{{ill|Mori Kōichi|ja|森浩一}}, ''Excavation of Kofun,'' Chūō Kōron Shinsho, 1965.. == Overview ==

Burial Method |- | rowspan=3 colspan=3 | || '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' || 84 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}} || 114 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun|ja|上侍塚北古墳}} || 48.5 m || South || None || Unknown |}
Shimosamuraizuka Kofun is located on a terrace on the right bank of the Naka River in northeastern Tochigi Prefecture. In addition to the Edo period excavation in 1692, another investigation was conducted in 1975. The tumulus is an anterior-posterior burial mound with the anterior section oriented to the south. Measuring 84 meters in length, it is the second largest of the six anterior-posterior burial mounds in the Nasu region. The mound is surrounded by a moat, where artifacts such as a red-painted earthenware jar with a perforated base have been discovered.{{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=1988|chapter=Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Place Names Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 9 Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490093}} The burial chamber, located at the top of the posterior section, is a simple clay-coffin burial with clay blocks placed at both ends. Artifacts recovered during the Edo period excavation include bronze mirrors (a Two Gods and Two Beasts Mirror), iron implements (15 sword fragments, armor pieces, a sword hilt, and a spearhead), and earthenware (four tall cups and a jar). The tumulus dates to the end of the 4th century, during the early Middle Kofun period. Following the Edo period investigation, pine trees were planted to protect the mound, making Shimosamuraizuka Kofun one of the earliest examples of cultural heritage preservation in Japan. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" designation.{{Cite web|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/|publisher=National Cultural Property Database}} == Mound Details == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun Information Board. * Total length: 84 meters * Posterior section: ** Length: 48 meters ** Width: 48 meters ** Height: 9.4 meters * Anterior section: ** Length: 36 meters ** Width: 36 meters ** Height: 5 meters == Samuraizuka Kofun Group == {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}} is a collection of burial mounds located northwest of Shimosamuraizuka Kofun. While around 10 mounds originally existed, only eight remain today and have been designated as municipal historic sites. The group consists of one anterior-posterior burial mound, six round mounds, and one square mound. Partial excavations have been conducted on mounds 5 and 8. File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, funcho.jpg|Posterior section mound top File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, kouhoubu.jpg|View from the anterior section toward the posterior section File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from the posterior section toward the anterior section File:下侍塚古墳出土 有段口辺壺.JPG|Stepped rim jar
{{small|Exhibited at Ōtawara City Nasu Fudoki-no-Oka Yuzukami Museum.}} File:下侍塚古墳 出土品.JPG|Haji ware
{{small|Exhibited at Ōtawara City Nasu Fudoki-no-Oka Yuzukami Museum.}} == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * Samuraizuka Kofun (includes Shimosamuraizuka and Kamisamuraizuka Kofun) – Designated on June 9, 1951. === Municipal Historic Sites === * Samuraizuka Kofun Group – Designated on February 15, 1966.[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082775061/ Municipal Historic Sites List, Ōtawara City]. == Related Facilities == * Ōtawara City Nasu Fudoki-no-Oka Museum – Houses and displays artifacts from Shimosamuraizuka Kofun and the Samuraizuka Kofun Group. == References == {{reflist}} == Related Reading == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=2013|chapter=|title=The Story of Nasu Fudoki – Exploring the Ancient Burial Mounds of Nasu|publisher=Ōtawara City Nasu Fudoki-no-Oka Museum}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun|ja|侍塚古墳}} == External Links == * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] – Ōtawara City * {{Commonscat|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun}} [[Category:Kofun in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ōtawara]] [[Category:Anterior-posterior burial mounds]] [[Category:National Historic Sites of Japan in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Shimousamurai Tumulus-qq]] [[Category:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:Shimo-Samurai-Tsuka Kofun]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:下侍塚古墳]]

{{Infobox historic site | name = Shimosamuraizuka Kofun | native_name = 下侍塚古墳 | native_language = ja | image = [[File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-3.jpg|280px]]
Mound (foreground: anterior section, background: posterior section) | other_name = Kurumazuka | location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|36|48|46.75|N|140|7|22.35|E|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳}} | built = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} | materials = {{ill|Clay-coffin burial|ja|粘土槨}} | excavation = 1692 (Edo period), 1975 | designation1 = National Historic Site of Japan | designation1_date = 1951 }} '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' (下侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後方墳}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site under the name {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun|ja|侍塚古墳}}. The tumulus is notable as one of the first burial mounds to be subjected to an academic archaeological investigation in Japan, conducted during the Edo period under the orders of Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Mito Kōmon). This article also covers the {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}}, a collection of tumuli near Shimosamuraizuka Kofun that is designated as a municipal historic site. It is known as "the most beautiful kofun in Japan"{{ill|Mori Kōichi|ja|森浩一}}, ''Excavation of Kofun,'' Chūō Kōron Shinsho, 1965.. == Overview ==

{{Infobox historic site | name = Shimosamuraizuka Kofun | native_name = 下侍塚古墳 | native_language = ja | image = [[File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-3.jpg|280px]]
Mound (foreground: anterior section, background: posterior section) | other_name = Kurumazuka | location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|36|48|46.75|N|140|7|22.35|E|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} | built = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} | materials = {{ill|Clay-coffin burial|ja|粘土槨}} | excavation = 1692 (Edo period), 1975 | designation1 = National Historic Site of Japan | designation1_date = 1951 }} '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' (下侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site under the name {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun|ja|侍塚古墳}}. The tumulus is notable as one of the first burial mounds to be subjected to an academic archaeological investigation in Japan, conducted during the Edo period under the orders of Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Mito Kōmon). This article also covers the {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}}, a collection of tumuli near Shimosamuraizuka Kofun that is designated as a municipal historic site. It is known as "the most beautiful kofun in Japan"{{ill|Mori Kōichi|ja|森浩一}}, ''Excavation of Kofun,'' Chūō Kōron Shinsho, 1965.. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Major Anterior-Posterior Burial Mounds in the Nasu Region ! colspan=3 | Tumulus Groups ! Tumulus Name ! Size ! Anterior Orientation ! Paving Stones ! Burial Method |- | rowspan=3 colspan=3 | || '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' || 84 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}} || 114 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun|ja|上侍塚北古墳}} || 48.5 m || South || None || Unknown |}
Shimosamuraizuka Kofun is located on a terrace on the right bank of the Naka River in northeastern Tochigi Prefecture. In addition to the Edo period excavation in 1692, another investigation was conducted in 1975. The tumulus is an anterior-posterior burial mound with the anterior section oriented to the south. Measuring 84 meters in length, it is the second largest of the six anterior-posterior burial mounds in the Nasu region. The mound is surrounded by a moat, where artifacts such as a red-painted earthenware jar with a perforated base have been discovered.{{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=1988|chapter=Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Place Names Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 9 Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490093}} The burial chamber, located at the top of the posterior section, is a simple clay-coffin burial with clay blocks placed at both ends. Artifacts recovered during the Edo period excavation include bronze mirrors (a Two Gods and Two Beasts Mirror), iron implements (15 sword fragments, armor pieces, a sword hilt, and a spearhead), and earthenware (four tall cups and a jar). The tumulus dates to the end of the 4th century, during the early Middle Kofun period. Following the Edo period investigation, pine trees were planted to protect the mound, making Shimosamuraizuka Kofun one of the earliest examples of cultural heritage preservation in Japan. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" designation.{{Cite web|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/|publisher=National Cultural Property Database}} == Mound Details == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun Information Board. * Total length: 84 meters * Posterior section: ** Length: 48 meters ** Width: 48 meters ** Height: 9.4 meters * Anterior section: ** Length: 36 meters ** Width: 36 meters ** Height: 5 meters == Samuraizuka Kofun Group == {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}} is a collection of burial mounds located northwest of Shimosamuraizuka Kofun. While around 10 mounds originally existed, only eight remain today and have been designated as municipal historic sites. The group consists of one anterior-posterior burial mound, six round mounds, and one square mound. Partial excavations have been conducted on mounds 5 and 8. File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, funcho.jpg|Posterior section mound top File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, kouhoubu.jpg|View from the anterior section toward the posterior section File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from the posterior section toward the anterior section File:下侍塚古墳出土 有段口辺壺.JPG|Stepped rim jar
{{small|Exhibited at Ōtawara City Nasu Fudoki-no-Oka Yuzukami Museum.}} File:下侍塚古墳 出土品.JPG|Haji ware

Anterior Orientation ! Paving Stones ! Burial Method |- | rowspan=3 colspan=3 | || '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' || 84 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}} || 114 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun|ja|上侍塚北古墳}} || 48.5 m || South || None || Unknown |}
Shimosamuraizuka Kofun is located on a terrace on the right bank of the Naka River in northeastern Tochigi Prefecture. In addition to the Edo period excavation in 1692, another investigation was conducted in 1975. The tumulus is an anterior-posterior burial mound with the anterior section oriented to the south. Measuring 84 meters in length, it is the second largest of the six anterior-posterior burial mounds in the Nasu region. The mound is surrounded by a moat, where artifacts such as a red-painted earthenware jar with a perforated base have been discovered.{{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=1988|chapter=Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Place Names Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 9 Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490093}} The burial chamber, located at the top of the posterior section, is a simple clay-coffin burial with clay blocks placed at both ends. Artifacts recovered during the Edo period excavation include bronze mirrors (a Two Gods and Two Beasts Mirror), iron implements (15 sword fragments, armor pieces, a sword hilt, and a spearhead), and earthenware (four tall cups and a jar). The tumulus dates to the end of the 4th century, during the early Middle Kofun period. Following the Edo period investigation, pine trees were planted to protect the mound, making Shimosamuraizuka Kofun one of the earliest examples of cultural heritage preservation in Japan. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" designation.{{Cite web|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/|publisher=National Cultural Property Database}} == Mound Details == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun Information Board. * Total length: 84 meters * Posterior section: ** Length: 48 meters ** Width: 48 meters ** Height: 9.4 meters * Anterior section: ** Length: 36 meters ** Width: 36 meters ** Height: 5 meters == Samuraizuka Kofun Group == {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}} is a collection of burial mounds located northwest of Shimosamuraizuka Kofun. While around 10 mounds originally existed, only eight remain today and have been designated as municipal historic sites. The group consists of one anterior-posterior burial mound, six round mounds, and one square mound. Partial excavations have been conducted on mounds 5 and 8. File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, funcho.jpg|Posterior section mound top File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, kouhoubu.jpg|View from the anterior section toward the posterior section File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from the posterior section toward the anterior section File:下侍塚古墳出土 有段口辺壺.JPG|Stepped rim jar
{{small|Exhibited at Ōtawara City Nasu Fudoki-no-Oka Yuzukami Museum.}} File:下侍塚古墳 出土品.JPG|Haji ware
{{small|Exhibited at Ōtawara City Nasu Fudoki-no-Oka Yuzukami Museum.}} == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * Samuraizuka Kofun (includes Shimosamuraizuka and Kamisamuraizuka Kofun) – Designated on June 9, 1951. === Municipal Historic Sites === * Samuraizuka Kofun Group – Designated on February 15, 1966.[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082775061/ Municipal Historic Sites List, Ōtawara City]. == Related Facilities == * Ōtawara City Nasu Fudoki-no-Oka Museum – Houses and displays artifacts from Shimosamuraizuka Kofun and the Samuraizuka Kofun Group. == References == {{reflist}} == Related Reading == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=2013|chapter=|title=The Story of Nasu Fudoki – Exploring the Ancient Burial Mounds of Nasu|publisher=Ōtawara City Nasu Fudoki-no-Oka Museum}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun|ja|侍塚古墳}} == External Links == * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] – Ōtawara City * {{Commonscat|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun}} [[Category:Kofun in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ōtawara]] [[Category:Anterior-posterior burial mounds]] [[Category:National Historic Sites of Japan in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-Shimousamurai Tumulus-qq]] [[Category:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:Shimo-Samurai-Tsuka Kofun]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:前方後方墳]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:前方後方墳]] [[ja:下侍塚古墳]]

{{Infobox historic site | name = Shimosamuraizuka Kofun | native_name = 下侍塚古墳 | native_language = ja | image = [[File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-3.jpg|280px]]
Mound (foreground: anterior section, background: posterior section) | other_name = Kurumazuka | location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|36|48|46.75|N|140|7|22.35|E|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} | built = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} | materials = {{ill|Clay-coffin burial|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} | excavation = 1692 (Edo period), 1975 | designation1 = National Historic Site of Japan | designation1_date = 1951 }} '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' (下侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site under the name {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun|ja|侍塚古墳}}. The tumulus is notable as one of the first burial mounds to be subjected to an academic archaeological investigation in Japan, conducted during the Edo period under the orders of Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Mito Kōmon). This article also covers the {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}}, a collection of tumuli near Shimosamuraizuka Kofun that is designated as a municipal historic site. It is known as "the most beautiful kofun in Japan"{{ill|Mori Kōichi|ja|森浩一}}, ''Excavation of Kofun,'' Chūō Kōron Shinsho, 1965.. == Overview ==

{{Infobox historic site | name = Shimosamuraizuka Kofun | native_name = 下侍塚古墳 | native_language = ja | image = [[File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-3.jpg|280px]]
Mound (foreground: anterior section, background: posterior section) | other_name = Kurumazuka | location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|36|48|46.75|N|140|7|22.35|E|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} | built = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} | materials = {{ill|Clay-coffin burial|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} | excavation = 1692 (Edo period), 1975 | designation1 = National Historic Site of Japan | designation1_date = 1951 }} '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' (下侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site under the name {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun|ja|侍塚古墳}}. The tumulus is notable as one of the first burial mounds to be subjected to an academic archaeological investigation in Japan, conducted during the Edo period under the orders of Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Mito Kōmon). This article also covers the {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}}, a collection of tumuli near Shimosamuraizuka Kofun that is designated as a municipal historic site. It is known as "the most beautiful kofun in Japan"{{ill|Mori Kōichi|ja|森浩一}}, ''Excavation of Kofun,'' Chūō Kōron Shinsho, 1965.. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Major Anterior-Posterior Burial Mounds in the Nasu Region ! colspan=3 | Tumulus Groups ! Tumulus Name ! Size ! Anterior Orientation ! Paving Stones ! Burial Method |- | rowspan=3 colspan=3 | || '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' || 84 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}} || 114 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun|ja|上侍塚北古墳}} || 48.5 m || South || None || Unknown |}
Shimosamuraizuka Kofun is located on a terrace on the right bank of the Naka River in northeastern Tochigi Prefecture. In addition to the Edo period excavation in 1692, another investigation was conducted in 1975. The tumulus is an anterior-posterior burial mound with the anterior section oriented to the south. Measuring 84 meters in length, it is the second largest of the six anterior-posterior burial mounds in the Nasu region. The mound is surrounded by a moat, where artifacts such as a red-painted earthenware jar with a perforated base have been discovered.{{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=1988|chapter=Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Place Names Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 9 Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490093}} The burial chamber, located at the top of the posterior section, is a simple clay-coffin burial with clay blocks placed at both ends. Artifacts recovered during the Edo period excavation include bronze mirrors (a Two Gods and Two Beasts Mirror), iron implements (15 sword fragments, armor pieces, a sword hilt, and a spearhead), and earthenware (four tall cups and a jar). The tumulus dates to the end of the 4th century, during the early Middle Kofun period. Following the Edo period investigation, pine trees were planted to protect the mound, making Shimosamuraizuka Kofun one of the earliest examples of cultural heritage preservation in Japan. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" designation.{{Cite web|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/|publisher=National Cultural Property Database}} == Mound Details == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun Information Board. * Total length: 84 meters * Posterior section: ** Length: 48 meters ** Width: 48 meters ** Height: 9.4 meters * Anterior section: ** Length: 36 meters ** Width: 36 meters ** Height: 5 meters == Samuraizuka Kofun Group == {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}} is a collection of burial mounds located northwest of Shimosamuraizuka Kofun. While around 10 mounds originally existed, only eight remain today and have been designated as municipal historic sites. The group consists of one anterior-posterior burial mound, six round mounds, and one square mound. Partial excavations have been conducted on mounds 5 and 8. File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, funcho.jpg|Posterior section mound top File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, kouhoubu.jpg|View from the anterior section toward the posterior section File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from the posterior section toward the anterior section File:下侍塚古墳出土 有段口辺壺.JPG|Stepped rim jar
{{small|Exhibited at Ōtawara City Nasu Fudoki-no-Oka Yuzukami Museum.}} File:下侍塚古墳 出土品.JPG|Haji ware

This article also covers the {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}}, a collection of tumuli near Shimosamuraizuka Kofun that is designated as a municipal historic site. It is known as "the most beautiful kofun in Japan"{{ill|Mori Kōichi|ja|森浩一}}, ''Excavation of Kofun,'' Chūō Kōron Shinsho, 1965.. == Overview ==

Shimosamuraizuka Kofun is located on a terrace on the right bank of the Naka River in northeastern Tochigi Prefecture. In addition to the Edo period excavation in 1692, another investigation was conducted in 1975. The tumulus is an anterior-posterior burial mound with the anterior section oriented to the south. Measuring 84 meters in length, it is the second largest of the six anterior-posterior burial mounds in the Nasu region. The mound is surrounded by a moat, where artifacts such as a red-painted earthenware jar with a perforated base have been discovered.{{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=1988|chapter=Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Place Names Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 9 Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490093}} The burial chamber, located at the top of the posterior section, is a simple clay-coffin burial with clay blocks placed at both ends. Artifacts recovered during the Edo period excavation include bronze mirrors (a Two Gods and Two Beasts Mirror), iron implements (15 sword fragments, armor pieces, a sword hilt, and a spearhead), and earthenware (four tall cups and a jar). The tumulus dates to the end of the 4th century, during the early Middle Kofun period. Following the Edo period investigation, pine trees were planted to protect the mound, making Shimosamuraizuka Kofun one of the earliest examples of cultural heritage preservation in Japan. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" designation.{{Cite web|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/|publisher=National Cultural Property Database}} == Mound Details == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun Information Board. * Total length: 84 meters * Posterior section: ** Length: 48 meters ** Width: 48 meters ** Height: 9.4 meters * Anterior section: ** Length: 36 meters ** Width: 36 meters ** Height: 5 meters == Samuraizuka Kofun Group == {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}} is a collection of burial mounds located northwest of Shimosamuraizuka Kofun. While around 10 mounds originally existed, only eight remain today and have been designated as municipal historic sites. The group consists of one anterior-posterior burial mound, six round mounds, and one square mound. Partial excavations have been conducted on mounds 5 and 8. {{Infobox historic site | name = Shimosamuraizuka Kofun | native_name = 下侍塚古墳 | native_language = ja | image = [[File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-3.jpg|280px]]
Mound (foreground: anterior section, background: posterior section) | other_name = Kurumazuka | location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|36|48|46.75|N|140|7|22.35|E|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} | built = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} | materials = {{ill|Clay-coffin burial|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} | excavation = 1692 (Edo period), 1975 | designation1 = National Historic Site of Japan | designation1_date = 1951 }} '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' (下侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site under the name {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun|ja|侍塚古墳}}. The tumulus is notable as one of the first burial mounds to be subjected to an academic archaeological investigation in Japan, conducted during the Edo period under the orders of Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Mito Kōmon). This article also covers the {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}}, a collection of tumuli near Shimosamuraizuka Kofun that is designated as a municipal historic site. It is known as "the most beautiful kofun in Japan"{{ill|Mori Kōichi|ja|森浩一}}, ''Excavation of Kofun,'' Chūō Kōron Shinsho, 1965.. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Major Anterior-Posterior Burial Mounds in the Nasu Region ! colspan=3 | Tumulus Groups ! Tumulus Name ! Size ! Anterior Orientation ! Paving Stones ! Burial Method |- | rowspan=3 colspan=3 | || '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' || 84 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}} || 114 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun|ja|上侍塚北古墳}} || 48.5 m || South || None || Unknown |}
Shimosamuraizuka Kofun is located on a terrace on the right bank of the Naka River in northeastern Tochigi Prefecture. In addition to the Edo period excavation in 1692, another investigation was conducted in 1975. The tumulus is an anterior-posterior burial mound with the anterior section oriented to the south. Measuring 84 meters in length, it is the second largest of the six anterior-posterior burial mounds in the Nasu region. The mound is surrounded by a moat, where artifacts such as a red-painted earthenware jar with a perforated base have been discovered.{{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=1988|chapter=Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Place Names Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 9 Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490093}} The burial chamber, located at the top of the posterior section, is a simple clay-coffin burial with clay blocks placed at both ends. Artifacts recovered during the Edo period excavation include bronze mirrors (a Two Gods and Two Beasts Mirror), iron implements (15 sword fragments, armor pieces, a sword hilt, and a spearhead), and earthenware (four tall cups and a jar). The tumulus dates to the end of the 4th century, during the early Middle Kofun period. Following the Edo period investigation, pine trees were planted to protect the mound, making Shimosamuraizuka Kofun one of the earliest examples of cultural heritage preservation in Japan. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" designation.{{Cite web|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/|publisher=National Cultural Property Database}} == Mound Details == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun Information Board. * Total length: 84 meters * Posterior section: ** Length: 48 meters ** Width: 48 meters ** Height: 9.4 meters * Anterior section: ** Length: 36 meters ** Width: 36 meters ** Height: 5 meters == Samuraizuka Kofun Group == {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}} is a collection of burial mounds located northwest of Shimosamuraizuka Kofun. While around 10 mounds originally existed, only eight remain today and have been designated as municipal historic sites. The group consists of one anterior-posterior burial mound, six round mounds, and one square mound. Partial excavations have been conducted on mounds 5 and 8. File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, funcho.jpg|Posterior section mound top File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, kouhoubu.jpg|View from the anterior section toward the posterior section File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from the posterior section toward the anterior section File:下侍塚古墳出土 有段口辺壺.JPG|Stepped rim jar
{{small|Exhibited at Ōtawara City Nasu Fudoki-no-Oka Yuzukami Museum.}} File:下侍塚古墳 出土品.JPG|Haji ware
{{small|Exhibited at Ōtawara City Nasu Fudoki-no-Oka Yuzukami Museum.}} == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * Samuraizuka Kofun (includes Shimosamuraizuka and Kamisamuraizuka Kofun) – Designated on June 9, 1951. === Municipal Historic Sites === * Samuraizuka Kofun Group – Designated on February 15, 1966.[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082775061/ Municipal Historic Sites List, Ōtawara City]. == Related Facilities == * Ōtawara City Nasu Fudoki-no-Oka Museum – Houses and displays artifacts from Shimosamuraizuka Kofun and the Samuraizuka Kofun Group. == References == {{reflist}} == Related Reading == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=2013|chapter=|title=The Story of Nasu Fudoki – Exploring the Ancient Burial Mounds of Nasu|publisher=Ōtawara City Nasu Fudoki-no-Oka Museum}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun|ja|侍塚古墳}} == External Links == * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] – Ōtawara City * {{Commonscat|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun}} [[Category:Kofun in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ōtawara]] [[Category:Anterior-posterior burial mounds]] [[Category:National Historic Sites of Japan in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:Shimo-Samurai-Tsuka Kofun]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:前方後方墳]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:前方後方墳]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:下侍塚古墳]] [[Category:New]]

{{Infobox historic site | name = Shimosamuraizuka Kofun | native_name = 下侍塚古墳 | native_language = ja | image = [[File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-3.jpg|280px]]
Mound (foreground: anterior section, background: posterior section) | other_name = Kurumazuka | location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|36|48|46.75|N|140|7|22.35|E|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} | built = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} | materials = {{ill|Clay-coffin burial|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} | excavation = 1692 (Edo period), 1975 | designation1 = National Historic Site of Japan | designation1_date = 1951 }} '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' (下侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site under the name {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun|ja|侍塚古墳}}. The tumulus is notable as one of the first burial mounds to be subjected to an academic archaeological investigation in Japan, conducted during the Edo period under the orders of Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Mito Kōmon). This article also covers the {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}}, a collection of tumuli near Shimosamuraizuka Kofun that is designated as a municipal historic site. It is known as "the most beautiful kofun in Japan"{{ill|Mori Kōichi|ja|森浩一}}, ''Excavation of Kofun,'' Chūō Kōron Shinsho, 1965.. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Major Anterior-Posterior Burial Mounds in the Nasu Region ! colspan=3 | Tumulus Groups ! Tumulus Name ! Size ! Anterior Orientation ! Paving Stones ! Burial Method |- | rowspan=3 colspan=3 | || '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' || 84 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}} || 114 m || South || Yes || Clay-coffin burial |- | {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka North Kofun|ja|上侍塚北古墳}} || 48.5 m || South || None || Unknown |}
Shimosamuraizuka Kofun is located on a terrace on the right bank of the Naka River in northeastern Tochigi Prefecture. In addition to the Edo period excavation in 1692, another investigation was conducted in 1975. The tumulus is an anterior-posterior burial mound with the anterior section oriented to the south. Measuring 84 meters in length, it is the second largest of the six anterior-posterior burial mounds in the Nasu region. The mound is surrounded by a moat, where artifacts such as a red-painted earthenware jar with a perforated base have been discovered.{{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=1988|chapter=Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Place Names Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 9 Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490093}} The burial chamber, located at the top of the posterior section, is a simple clay-coffin burial with clay blocks placed at both ends. Artifacts recovered during the Edo period excavation include bronze mirrors (a Two Gods and Two Beasts Mirror), iron implements (15 sword fragments, armor pieces, a sword hilt, and a spearhead), and earthenware (four tall cups and a jar). The tumulus dates to the end of the 4th century, during the early Middle Kofun period. Following the Edo period investigation, pine trees were planted to protect the mound, making Shimosamuraizuka Kofun one of the earliest examples of cultural heritage preservation in Japan. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" designation.{{Cite web|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/|publisher=National Cultural Property Database}} == Mound Details == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun Information Board. * Total length: 84 meters * Posterior section: ** Length: 48 meters ** Width: 48 meters ** Height: 9.4 meters * Anterior section: ** Length: 36 meters ** Width: 36 meters ** Height: 5 meters == Samuraizuka Kofun Group == {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}} is a collection of burial mounds located northwest of Shimosamuraizuka Kofun. While around 10 mounds originally existed, only eight remain today and have been designated as municipal historic sites. The group consists of one anterior-posterior burial mound, six round mounds, and one square mound. Partial excavations have been conducted on mounds 5 and 8. File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, funcho.jpg|Posterior section mound top File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, kouhoubu.jpg|View from the anterior section toward the posterior section File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from the posterior section toward the anterior section File:下侍塚古墳出土 有段口辺壺.JPG|Stepped rim jar
{{small|Exhibited at Ōtawara City Nasu Fudoki-no-Oka Yuzukami Museum.}} File:下侍塚古墳 出土品.JPG|Haji ware
{{small|Exhibited at Ōtawara City Nasu Fudoki-no-Oka Yuzukami Museum.}} == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * Samuraizuka Kofun (includes Shimosamuraizuka and Kamisamuraizuka Kofun) – Designated on June 9, 1951. === Municipal Historic Sites === * Samuraizuka Kofun Group – Designated on February 15, 1966.[https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082775061/ Municipal Historic Sites List, Ōtawara City]. == Related Facilities == * Ōtawara City Nasu Fudoki-no-Oka Museum – Houses and displays artifacts from Shimosamuraizuka Kofun and the Samuraizuka Kofun Group. == References == {{reflist}} == Related Reading == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=2013|chapter=|title=The Story of Nasu Fudoki – Exploring the Ancient Burial Mounds of Nasu|publisher=Ōtawara City Nasu Fudoki-no-Oka Museum}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun|ja|侍塚古墳}} == External Links == * [https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/ Samuraizuka Kofun] – Ōtawara City * {{Commonscat|Shimosamuraizuka Kofun}} [[Category:Kofun in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ōtawara]] [[Category:Anterior-posterior burial mounds]] [[Category:National Historic Sites of Japan in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:Shimo-Samurai-Tsuka Kofun]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:前方後方墳]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:前方後方墳]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shimousamurai Tumulus]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:下侍塚古墳]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] Measuring 84 meters in length, it is the second largest of the six anterior-posterior burial mounds in the Nasu region. The mound is surrounded by a moat, where artifacts such as a red-painted earthenware jar with a perforated base have been discovered.{{Cite book|editor=|author=Ōtawara City|year=1988|chapter=Shimosamuraizuka Kofun|title={{ill|Japanese Historical Place Names Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 9 Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490093}} The burial chamber, located at the top of the posterior section, is a simple clay-coffin burial with clay blocks placed at both ends. Artifacts recovered during the Edo period excavation include bronze mirrors (a Two Gods and Two Beasts Mirror), iron implements (15 sword fragments, armor pieces, a sword hilt, and a spearhead), and earthenware (four tall cups and a jar). The tumulus dates to the end of the 4th century, during the early Middle Kofun period. Following the Edo period investigation, pine trees were planted to protect the mound, making Shimosamuraizuka Kofun one of the earliest examples of cultural heritage preservation in Japan. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in 1951 as part of the "Samuraizuka Kofun" designation.{{Cite web|title=Samuraizuka Kofun|url=https://www.city.ohtawara.tochigi.jp/docs/2013082767660/|publisher=National Cultural Property Database}} == Mound Details == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun Information Board. * Total length: 84 meters * Posterior section: ** Length: 48 meters ** Width: 48 meters ** Height: 9.4 meters * Anterior section: ** Length: 36 meters ** Width: 36 meters ** Height: 5 meters == Samuraizuka Kofun Group == {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}} is a collection of burial mounds located northwest of Shimosamuraizuka Kofun. While around 10 mounds originally existed, only eight remain today and have been designated as municipal historic sites. The group consists of one anterior-posterior burial mound, six round mounds, and one square mound. Partial excavations have been conducted on mounds 5 and 8. {{Infobox historic site | name = Shimosamuraizuka Kofun | native_name = 下侍塚古墳 | native_language = ja | image = [[File:Shimosamuraizuka Kofun, funkyu-3.jpg|280px]]
Mound (foreground: anterior section, background: posterior section) | other_name = Kurumazuka | location = {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|36|48|46.75|N|140|7|22.35|E|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} | built = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} | materials = {{ill|Clay-coffin burial|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} | excavation = 1692 (Edo period), 1975 | designation1 = National Historic Site of Japan | designation1_date = 1951 }} '''Shimosamuraizuka Kofun''' (下侍塚古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} located in {{ill|Ōtawara|ja|大田原市}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, Japan. Together with {{ill|Kamisamuraizuka Kofun|ja|上侍塚古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site under the name {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun|ja|侍塚古墳}}. The tumulus is notable as one of the first burial mounds to be subjected to an academic archaeological investigation in Japan, conducted during the Edo period under the orders of Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Mito Kōmon). This article also covers the {{ill|Samuraizuka Kofun Group|ja|侍塚古墳群}}, a collection of tumuli near Shimosamuraizuka Kofun that is designated as a municipal historic site. It is known as "the most beautiful kofun in Japan"{{ill|Mori Kōichi|ja|森浩一}}, ''Excavation of Kofun,'' Chūō Kōron Shinsho, 1965.. == Overview ==
